<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- High coupling (0.57) — 3 imports target "analyzers-2"
- High coupling (0.57) — 3 imports target "file-analysis-pipeline"
- src/schema/validate.ts is the sole production file in this community; its clustering with e2e and integration tests suggests it is almost exclusively exercised at system boundaries rather than unit-tested in isolation — add dedicated unit tests for edge cases.
- The mixing of e2e, integration, and unit schema tests in one community points to a test organisation gap; grouping by test level (unit/integration/e2e directories) is already the project convention, so verify the schema unit test is in the right place.
- This zone imports 3 symbols from the File Analysis Pipeline zone, meaning end-to-end tests depend on internal analyzer APIs — prefer importing through the public API (src/public.ts) to insulate tests from internal refactors.
- validate.ts is a production module that was absorbed into a test-dominated community; this is a naming and classification risk — the zone correctly carries a production name to avoid tooling treating validate.ts as test-only code.
- E2E tests for both 'cli-analyze' and 'cli-serve' commands live here alongside schema unit tests, creating a zone with mixed abstraction levels; splitting CLI e2e tests into the CLI Infrastructure zone would improve cohesion.
- Cohesion of 0.43 reflects the heterogeneous mix of production schema code and multi-level tests; resolving the test placement issue described above would likely push cohesion above 0.5.
- The 3 imports from this zone into file-analysis-pipeline (e2e → analyzers-2) combined with the 2 imports into the e2e zone from the branch-work-analytics zone (unit → e2e) creates a transitive coupling chain: branch-work-analytics → schema-validation → file-analysis-pipeline. This indirect path means analytics-layer changes can propagate through test code into file-analysis-pipeline unexpectedly.
- validate.ts is the sole production file yet has a cohesion score (0.43) dragged down by heterogeneous test neighbors — its actual coupling profile is obscured by co-membership with e2e tests, making it hard to assess whether the production schema validation boundary is healthy or leaky.
- Transitive coupling chain exists: branch-work-analytics (unit) → schema-validation (e2e) → file-analysis-pipeline (analyzers-2). Changes in file-analysis-pipeline internals can silently break branch-work-analytics tests via this three-zone indirect path.
- validate.ts cohesion is obscured by co-membership with multi-level test files; isolating it in a dedicated schema zone would surface its true coupling profile and make the production schema boundary auditable.
- Zone-level metrics for schema-validation are computed across 1 production file and 4 test files spanning three test levels (unit, integration, e2e). The reported cohesion (0.43) and coupling (0.57) reflect the mixed population, not validate.ts's true production coupling profile. Any threshold-based alerts or architectural trend analysis applied to this zone will be systematically misleading until the test files are removed from this community.
- schema-validation aggregates validate.ts with 4 test files at three different abstraction levels in a single community. Zone health metrics are dominated by the test file population, making validate.ts's actual production coupling invisible in aggregate reports. The production file should reside in a dedicated schema zone; the test files should be distributed to their respective test-level zones.
- schema-validation is the only zone that contains tests from three different abstraction levels (unit, integration, e2e) alongside a production file. The other production zones (file-analysis-pipeline, zone-detection-engine) contain only same-level (unit) tests. This implicit convention — production zones may co-locate unit tests but not integration or e2e tests — is followed everywhere except schema-validation, indicating the violation is accidental rather than intentional and should be codified as an explicit rule.
- The zone name 'schema-validation' accurately describes the production file (validate.ts) but is simultaneously an accurate description of what the co-located tests do. This naming coincidence means the zone name provides no signal about whether a given file is production or test code, further obscuring the boundary.
- schema-validation is the only zone with both high coupling (0.57) AND low cohesion (0.43) AND mixed-abstraction-level test membership simultaneously. This triple combination makes it the highest-risk zone in the four visible here — not because any single metric is catastrophically bad, but because all three risk indicators co-occur. Prioritize decomposing this zone above the others.
- Codify the implicit convention surfaced by this zone: production zones may co-locate unit tests (as file-analysis-pipeline and zone-detection-engine do) but must not contain integration or e2e tests. Adding a lint or zone-membership rule enforcing this boundary would prevent schema-validation's pattern from recurring in new zones.
- Coupling to 'analyzers-2' reflects intra-domain edges within the Louvain-fragmented analyzers cluster, not a structural dependency risk
- Zone finding 0 (warning: coupling 0.57 to 'analyzers-2') is contradicted by global finding 10. E2e tests coupling to what is semantically one analyzers domain is expected; the warning severity is not justified. The coupling flag measures intra-domain edges that Louvain partitioned into separate zone identifiers, not genuine cross-concern dependencies.
